policy_statement: Convert 110 tourist cruise boats and 155 small passenger boats to run by natural gas.
mechanism_description: The plan is to convert cruise and small passenger boats to run on natural gas, which shifts marine fuel use and reduces vessel emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel switch in transport → ↓ CO₂e per unit propulsion (combustion + upstream) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human activity/system changed: marine passenger vessels’ propulsion fuel is switched from conventional marine fuels to natural gas (often LNG/CNG) via engine conversion or replacement and related fueling logistics. (2) Mediating physical process: lower CO₂ emitted per unit energy from natural gas combustion and potentially higher engine efficiency reduce CO₂; however, methane leakage and engine “methane slip” can add CH₄, affecting net CO₂e. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: net reduction (or in edge cases, increase) in total CO₂e emissions from vessel operations over the fuel cycle relative to the baseline fuel.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from marine propulsion by replacing higher-carbon marine fuels with natural gas (subject to methane slip).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: a transport fuel switch changes fuel carbon intensity/combustion characteristics, primarily aiming to lower operational and lifecycle GHG emissions from vessels.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: although the switch can also reduce local air pollutants and sometimes improve energy efficiency, the main climate-relevant intent and effect is emissions mitigation (CO₂e reduction), not resource efficiency as the dominant framing.  
3) Edge cases applied: if methane slip and upstream leakage are high, LNG/CNG can erode or negate GHG benefits; nevertheless, the mechanism remains a mitigation mechanism because it is fundamentally an emissions-reduction lever (fuel substitution) rather than adaptation, resource efficiency, or nature-based action.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy that “switches marine vessel fuel to natural gas” shares the same primary mitigation pathway of altering fuel-based emissions from marine transport.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether it is best tagged as “fuel switching,” “low-carbon fuels,” or “transport decarbonization” and the magnitude/sign of benefits depends on vessel type, engine tech, duty cycle, and methane controls—these require policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Methane slip (especially in some dual-fuel engines) and upstream leakage can substantially reduce or reverse net GHG benefits versus diesel; classification remains Mitigation, but expected impact direction/magnitude is context-dependent and should be validated with lifecycle assumptions and engine specifications.
key_indicators: "ambition is to convert"; "convert 110 cruise boats"; "155 small passengers boats"
co_benefits: Air quality improvements (lower SOx/PM/NOx vs some marine fuels); potential noise reduction
instance_edge_case_notes: Fuel type (natural gas/LNG/CNG) is implied by the policy_statement but not explicitly stated in the verbatim text; also, net GHG benefit depends on methane leakage/methane slip across the fuel cycle.
